narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 55.5% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 34, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both at 3, middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27, undergraduate GPA is 3.24, and decile rankings are notably higher at around 6 for Year 1 and 5.9 for Year 3, showing how your profile stacks up against those who succeeded. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your Year 3 decile ranking, your Year 1 decile ranking, and—counterbalancing them somewhat—your LSAT score. Your Year 3 decile of 3 had the strongest pull toward predicting bar failure, as it signals performance below what the model associates with bar success in later undergraduate years. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 3 reinforced that concern, highlighting early academic standing that's lower than typical passers. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 34 worked against a failure prediction, nudging the model toward seeing potential for passing despite the other factors. Your middle family income aligns with about a third of bar passers, and your full-time status matches the vast majority of them, so those played smaller roles here. Overall, the model saw your decile rankings as red flags compared to admitted students, outweighing your solid LSAT and other strengths. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your grades to improve future decile rankings, or exploring programs with bridge courses or bar prep support tailored for full-time students like you. Gathering letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience or any upward trends in your academics could also help reframe your story in a future application. With targeted improvements, you can shift this prediction in your favor and pursue your legal ambitions.
